title: Topologically protected long-range correlations in steady states of driven-dissipative bosonic chains
authors: Miguel Clavero Rubio, Tomás Ramos, Diego Porras
abstract: Driven-dissipative quantum systems can exhibit robust transport and amplification in topological regimes, yet the connection between topology and the extent of correlations remains largely unexplored. In this work, we develop a general framework that links topological phases in driven-dissipative systems to bosonic correlations via the singular value decomposition (SVD). In essence, we claim that non-Hermitian topology in quadratic Liouvillians is directly encoded in steady-state correlations, providing an intrinsic characterization of topology without external probes. We show that topological amplification induces disorder-robust long-range order (LRO) in steady-state correlations at fixed frequency, establishing frequency-resolved correlations as direct signatures of non-Hermitian topological phases. We introduce a vector-valued topological invariant that captures the total number of singular-value gap closings across the frequency axis, extending the concept of adiabatic deformation from topological insulators to the case of topological phases of quadratic Liouvillians. Within this framework, we further demonstrate that the spatial structure of equal-time correlations encodes global topological information, manifested as a Gaussian spatial decay with distance in the topological phase, in contrast to the exponential decay characteristic of trivial phases. These findings open new avenues for quantum sensing and correlation engineering in non-Hermitian systems, with feasible implementations in platforms such as trapped ions and superconducting circuits.
summary: This paper reveals that the topology of driven-dissipative bosonic chains is directly imprinted on their steady-state correlations. It shows that topological phases exhibit robust long-range order in the frequency domain and a unique Gaussian spatial decay. This provides a way to identify non-Hermitian topological phases using only intrinsic system properties.
main_problem: The study aims to establish a direct link between non-Hermitian topology and the spatial/spectral extent of correlations in driven-dissipative bosonic systems without requiring external probes.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that topological phases in quadratic Liouvillians are encoded in steady-state correlations, manifesting as frequency-resolved long-range order and a transition from exponential to Gaussian spatial decay in equal-time correlations.
method: The work utilizes Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of the dynamical matrix, the Quantum Langevin formalism, and a new vector-valued topological invariant to characterize the system.
model_or_system: The study focuses on driven-dissipative bosonic chains described by a Lindblad master equation, specifically analyzing non-interacting/quadratic models like the dissipative BdG chain and a dimerized dissipative Kitaev chain.
key_observables: Frequency-resolved correlations, equal-time correlations (normal and anomalous), singular-value spectrum, winding number array, and the LRO parameter.
important_parameters: Dissipation rates (gamma), hopping (J), parametric coupling (g), disorder strength (W), and system size (N).
main_assumptions: The analysis primarily focuses on quadratic (non-interacting) Liouvillians and assumes the validity of the thermodynamic limit for certain scaling laws.
figures_summary: Figures establish the diagnostic power of correlations (showing LRO vs. exponential decay), illustrate the physical models (BdG and dimerized chains), and present numerical/analytical comparisons of singular values and vectors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of topological diagnostics, develops an SVD-based framework for correlations, applies this to specific bosonic models, investigates the impact of disorder and scaling in the thermodynamic limit, and concludes with potential experimental implementations.
why_interesting: It provides a novel, intrinsic way to detect non-Hermitian topology through steady-state observables, which is highly relevant for researchers working on quantum sensing, non-equilibrium phase transitions, and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic representation of normalized frequency-resolved ( ¯ Nij) and equal-time ( ¯ Nij) correlations in topological and trivial regimes. In the topological phase, frequency-resolved correlations exhibit long-range order, while equal-time correlations display a Gaussian decay. In the trivial regime, both correlations decay exponentially with distance. These results establish two-point correlations as a key steady-state observable for diagnosing non-Hermitian topology. The frequency-resolved topological invariant is denoted by ν, while steady-state topology is characterized by the winding-number array ⃗V. Both observables and invariants are defined below.

Figure 2. Schematic representation of the dissipative bosonic Kitaev chain. Each site has onsite energy ∆, single-site parametric term gs, and local losses γ. Neighboring sites are connected by non-reciprocal complex hopping Je±iϕ (blue arrows), and parametric couplings gc (red arrows).

Figure 3. Top: Dimerized version of the dissipative bosonic Kitaev chain. Even (blue) sites correspond to the main chain, characterized by onsite energy ∆, non-reciprocal complex hopping Je±iϕ (blue arrows), local (gs) and nearest-neighbor (gc) parametric couplings (red arrows), and local losses γ. Odd (red) sites represent auxiliary modes, coupled to the main chain via g′ c, and subject to much stronger dissipation γ′ ≫γ. Bottom: Upon adiabatic elimination of the auxiliary sites, the system maps onto an effective bosonic chain equivalent to Model I, featuring collective gain terms Pjl (yellow arrows).

Figure 4. (a) Model I: Topological phase diagram for the symmetric parameter set defined in Sec. 4.1. (b) Model II: Topological phase diagram for with gs/g = gc/g = 0.1, g′ c/g = 3 and γ′/g = 30 (corresponding to P/g = 3

Figure 5. Model I: Singular values as a function of the frequency ω for PBC. (a) Topologically non-trivial phase with ⃗ Va = (0, 1, 0, 1, 0), for γ/g = 1.6. (b) Topologically non-trivial phase with ⃗Vb = (0, 1, 0), for γ/g = 4. (c) Critical point with ⃗Vc = (0, 0), for γc/g = 6. (d) Trivial phase with ⃗Vd = (0), for γ/g = 8. Each component of ⃗V indicate the topological winding numbers associated with distinct frequency intervals. For example, ⃗ Va = (0, 1, 0, 1, 0) corresponds to alternating regions of trivial and non-trivial topology, separated by singular value gap closings, while ⃗Vb = (0, 1, 0) indicates a single non-trivial frequency band flanked by trivial regions.
